Test Report of Performance of Version 3 RAMSESII-SNPP 
Retrievals 

Prepared by Jacola Roman, Tao Wang, Qing Yue, and Sun Wong 

Contributors: Bjorn Lambrigtsen, Mathias Schreier, Ruth Monarrez, Evan Manning, Eric Fetzer 

Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of Technology 

RAMSES II Product Version v03_20_02 (algorithm version V3.2.0) 

This assessment was carried out at the Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of Technology, 
under a contract with the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (80NM0018D0004). 

December 2022 

Goddard Earth Sciences Data and Information Services Center (GES DISC) 
http://disc.gsfc.nasa.gov 
NASA Goddard Space Flight Center  
Code 610.2  
Greenbelt, MD 20771 USA 
© 2022. All rights reserved.

2 

 
 
 
 
 1.  Technical Summary 

This report assesses the general quality of the Level 2 (L2) products from the Version 3 
RAMSES II (Retrieval Algorithm for Microwave Sounders in Earth Science) retrieval system 
for ATMS (Advanced Technology Microwave Sounder) onboard the SNPP (Suomi-National 
Polar-orbiting  Partnership)  satellite  (referred  to  in  this  report  as  RAMSESII-SNPP).  The 
specific  algorithm version tested is  version 03-20-02, herein referred to as V3.  This second 
release of RAMSES II is an early research version intended to solicit user comments.  

RAMSES II is a single-footprint MW-Only retrieval system for ATMS, which applies an 
optimal  estimation  algorithm  performing  “all-sky”  retrievals  based  solely  on  Microwave 
(MW) sounder data independent of cloud coverage. RAMSESII uses background information 
from MERRA2 (Modern-Era Retrospective Analysis for Research and Applications version 
2) along with a radiative transfer algorithm and a non-linear solver to achieve an optimized 
retrieval of profiles of atmospheric states. Uncertainty estimates and quality flags are based on 
optimization and convergence between observed and calculated brightness temperatures.  

ATMS  onboard  the  SNPP satellite  is  a  cross-track  scanner  with  22  channels  in  spectral 
bands  from  23  GHz  to  183  GHz,  providing  MW  measurements  in  both  clear  and  cloudy 
conditions. ATMS provides both temperature (T) soundings (between the surface and the upper 
stratosphere) and humidity (q) soundings (between the surface and upper troposphere). ATMS 
has better sampling and two more channels than its predecessor AMSU (Advanced Microwave 
Sounding Unit). SNPP is a polar-orbiting satellite launched in 2011 that crosses the equator 
about 14 times daily at 13:30 local time in ascending node and 1:30 local time in descending 
node, providing full global coverage twice a day. Acting as the bridge between NASA's Earth 
Observing System and the Joint Polar Satellite System (JPSS), SNPP is the first in a series of 
five next-generation U.S. weather satellites of the JPSS and is a result of a partnership between 
NOAA, NASA, and the Department of Defense (DoD).  

This report aims to test the performance of V3 RAMSESII-SNPP L2 retrievals. The data 
can  be  downloaded  from  the  Goddard  Distributed  Active  Archive  Center  (GDAAC).  The 
testing  report  aims  to  summarize  the  general  quality  of  several  core  products  of  the 
RAMSESII-SNPP L2 retrievals by the following analyses: 1) evaluating the retrieval yield for 
atmospheric T and H2O profiles; 2) quantifying the biases and root mean square errors (RMSE) 
of T and H2O retrievals by pixel-scale  comparisons to collocated radiosonde  measurements 
from the Integrated Global Radiosonde Archive (IGRA, over land), Alfred Wegener Institute 
(AWI) Polarstern laboratory (over the ocean), as well as the 5th generation global atmospheric 
reanalysis from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ERA5, both land 
and ocean).  

2.  Data and Methodology 
2.1  RAMSESII-SNPP V3 Retrieval Products 

This  test  version  of  RAMSES  II,  applied  to  ATMS  onboard  both  SNPP  and  J1,  is 
v03_20_02, herein V3. More details on the retrieval algorithm can be found in the RAMSESII 

3 

 
 
 Retrieval Algorithm for Microwave Sounders in Earth Science “The NASA ATMS Retrieval 
System Level 2 Algorithm Theoretical Basis Document (ATBD)”. 

RAMSESII final retrievals of T and H2O profiles are originally reported at 72 sigma levels 
following the topography. In generating the unified products they are further interpolated at 
100 and 66 pressure levels, respectively. The H2O profiles are specific humidity defined as the 
mass fraction of water vapor in moist air in units of kg/kg. 

For  both  T  and  H2O  retrievals,  RAMSESII  L2  provides  associated  quality  control 
indicators  with  the  names  “_qc”  appended  to  each  variable,  where  QC=0  indicates  the  best 
retrievals  that  meet  the  accuracy  requirements;  QC=1  indicates  good  retrievals,  and  QC=2  
suggests the use of such data is not recommended. The quality flag for RAMSESII is based on 
a threshold on the convergence using a two-step process. For more information on this two-
step process, please see the ATBD.  

T and H2O analyses are aggregated by spatial coverage as well as the associated surface 
type,  surface  temperature,  and  total  column  water  vapor  from  the  RAMSESII-SNPP  L2 
product. This will provide insight into the quality of RAMSESII-SNPP retrievals in different 
climate regimes and physical conditions. Single-level variables such as 2-meter air temperature, 
surface skin temperature,  and sea ice fraction are also retrieved. These variables along with 
total column water vapor (TCWV) will be  examined and assessed. 2-meter air temperature, 
surface skin temperature, and TCWV have corresponding quality control flags that follow the 
same format as those for the T and H2O profiles. Surface temperature does have a first guess 
from MERRA2, but other regression estimates, such as rain rates, 2-meter surface temperature, 
and ice/snow coverage,  are solely estimated on channel differences.  All variables presented 
here  are  reported  on  the  135  Along-track  Field  Of  View  (FOV)  and  96  Cross-Track  FOV 
spatial dimensions for each granule. The detailed file format and definitions of variables in the 
RAMSESII-SNPP files can be found in the product user guide [Monarrez et al. 2021]. 

2.2 IGRA radiosondes 

To  evaluate  the  performance  of  RAMSESII-SNPP  retrieval  products,  8  months 
(Jan/Apr/Jul/Oct of 2015 and 2019) of RAMSESII-SNPP retrieved L2 T and H2O profiles were 
collocated to radiosonde observations of the version 2 Integrated Global Radiosonde Archive 
(IGRA) [Durre and Yin, 2008] using the nearest neighbor approach with temporal and spatial 
tolerances of 3 hours and 50 km, respectively. The v2 IGRA consists of radiosonde and pilot 
balloon observations at over 2,700 globally distributed stations – mostly over land. The period 
availability of the IGRA archive varies from station to station. Previously, the IGRA dataset 
has been used to investigate the cloud-induced uncertainties in AIRS version 6 data [Wong et 
al., 2015]. 

2.3 AWI radiosondes 

RAMSESII-SNPP retrievals were also collocated to radiosondes available from the Alfred 
Wegener Institute (AWI) Polarstern laboratory [König-Langlo and Marx, 1997; Driemel et al., 

4 

 
 
 
 2016]. The AWI Polarstern has been operated since 1982, and it  was the first research ship 
ever to cross the Atlantic Ocean in a meridional section twice a year. It is therefore a useful 
validation dataset for data-sparse regions such as over the oceans and at the poles. 

2.4 ERA5 reanalysis 

The ERA5 is the 5th generation global atmospheric reanalysis from the European Centre 
for  Medium-Range  Weather  Forecasts  (ECMWF),  replacing  the  ERA-Interim  reanalysis 
which stopped being produced on August 31st, 2019. Produced using 4D-Var data assimilation, 
the ERA5 has a much higher spatial and temporal resolution than its predecessor, the ERA-
Interim.  In  addition,  newly  reprocessed  datasets  along  with  recent  instruments  have  been 
assimilated  into  the  ERA5  that  could  not  be  ingested  into  the  ERA-Interim  [Hennermann 
2020].  

The ERA5 outputs at various temporal resolutions. For the closest matchup, this study used 
the hourly output of temperature and specific humidity profiles with 37 pressure levels on a 
latitude-longitude  grid  of  0.25ox0.25o.  These  gridded  products  were  extracted  from  the 
Copernicus Climate Change Service (C3S) Climate Data Store from January 1st-5th 2015, July 
1st-5th 2015, and January 1st – 5th 2019 [Copernicus 2017].  

Profiles were collocated to RAMSESII-SNPP using a nearest-neighbor approach with the 

following requirements: 

•  1-hour temporal differences 
•  1o latitude/longitude radius 

Temperature and specific humidity profiles that were collocated to RAMSESII-SNPP were 
vertically  log/log  interpolated  to  the  RAMSESII-SNPP  pressure  levels.  The  matchups  were 
aggregated  by  month  (January  or  July  2015),  quality  control  flags  (QC),  spatial  coverage 
(global, zonal, and maps), and platform (J1 or SNPP for January 2019).  

2.5 Testing metrics 

The bias and root mean square error (RMSE) will be used to assess the differences between 
RAMSESII-SNPP retrievals to reference datasets. RAMSESII-SNPP L2 profiles of T and H2O 
will be compared to IGRA and AWI (section 3.2) and ERA5 (section 3.3). For temperature, 
the bias is calculated using the following formula: 

Similarly, the RMSE for temperature is defined as: 

𝑇𝑏𝑖𝑎𝑠 = 𝑚𝑒𝑎𝑛(𝑇𝑟𝑒𝑡𝑟𝑖𝑒𝑣𝑎𝑙 − 𝑇𝑟𝑒𝑓𝑒𝑟𝑒𝑛𝑐𝑒)             (1) 

𝑇𝑅𝑀𝑆𝐸 = √𝑚𝑒𝑎𝑛((𝑇𝑟𝑒𝑡𝑟𝑖𝑒𝑣𝑎𝑙 − 𝑇𝑟𝑒𝑓𝑒𝑟𝑒𝑛𝑐𝑒)

2

)                  (2) 

Because of the large variability of H2O from the surface to the upper troposphere, in most 
cases the H2O bias is calculated relative to the mean state of the reference using the following 
equation: 

𝐻2𝑂𝑏𝑖𝑎𝑠 =

𝑚𝑒𝑎𝑛(𝐻2𝑂𝑟𝑒𝑡𝑟𝑖𝑒𝑣𝑎𝑙−𝐻2𝑂𝑟𝑒𝑓𝑒𝑟𝑒𝑛𝑐𝑒)
𝑚𝑒𝑎𝑛(𝐻2𝑂𝑟𝑒𝑓𝑒𝑟𝑒𝑛𝑐𝑒)

 ×  100            (3) 

Similarly, the RMSE will be normalized for specific humidity using the following formula: 

5 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
                   𝐻2𝑂𝑅𝑀𝑆𝐸 =

2
√𝑚𝑒𝑎𝑛((𝐻2𝑂𝑟𝑒𝑡𝑟𝑖𝑒𝑣𝑎𝑙−𝐻2𝑂𝑟𝑒𝑓𝑒𝑟𝑒𝑛𝑐𝑒)

)

𝑚𝑒𝑎𝑛(𝐻2𝑂𝑟𝑒𝑓𝑒𝑟𝑒𝑛𝑐𝑒)

× 100      (4) 

Both temperature and H2O will be conditioned by the quality control flags (QC) and other 
variables. Global maps at selected pressure levels, global means, as well as zonal means, will 
be presented to highlight spatial differences. Similar definitions of bias and RMSE are used 
for other retrieved variables, please see the details discussed in the following sections. 

2.6 Skill Score of The Final Retrieval Against Reference Datasets 

A  skill  score  test  was  calculated  to  assess  whether  the  RAMSESII-SNPP  retrieval 
outperforms the first guess (MERRA2) – when compared to the common reference datasets 
from IGRA or ERA5. Skill score is a forecasting metric that allows a user to test whether one 
forecast has more skill than another [Murphy, 1988; WMO, 2012]. The formula for the mean 
squared error skill score is as follows: 

                𝑆𝑘𝑖𝑙𝑙 𝑆𝑐𝑜𝑟𝑒 = 1 −

𝑀𝑆𝐸𝑅𝐴𝑀𝑆𝐸𝑆𝐼𝐼
𝑀𝑆𝐸𝐹𝐺

= 1 −

𝑚𝑒𝑎𝑛((𝑇𝑅𝐴𝑀𝑆𝐸𝑆𝐼𝐼−𝑇𝑟𝑒𝑓𝑒𝑟𝑒𝑛𝑐𝑒)2)
𝑚𝑒𝑎𝑛((𝑇 𝐹𝐺−𝑇𝑟𝑒𝑓𝑒𝑟𝑒𝑛𝑐𝑒)2)

         (5) 

The skill score for H2O uses the same formula. Please note that this metric depends highly 
on what is considered the reference, which in this case is either IGRA or ERA5. Note that the 
skill  score  can  also  be  calculated  using  RMSE  instead  of  MSE  as  long  as  the  metric  is 
consistent for both the nominator and the denominator. The skill score presented in the IGRA 
comparison used RMSE instead of MSE.  

If the skill score is greater than 0, meaning that the MSE/RMSE of the RAMSESII-SNPP 
is smaller than the MSE/RMSE of its FG (in this case MERRA2) when compared to the same 
reference datasets (IGRA or ERA5), and therefore in terms of retrievability the RAMSESII-
SNPP has more skill than its FG; and vice versa. The value of the skill score represents how 
much the MSE/RMSE was reduced or increased. For example, a skill score value of 0.25 means 
RAMSESII-SNPP reduced the MSE/RMSE observed in the FG by 25%.  

3.  Results 

In section 3.1 below we discuss the RAMSESII-SNPP retrieval yield, which represents the 
percentage of data bearing different quality controls (QC) with respect to  a total number of 
retrievals. In section 3.2, L2 RAMSESII-SNPP profiles collocated to sondes will be analyzed 
while  those  collocated  to  ERA5  will  be  discussed  in  section  3.3.  Although  not  shown,  an 
analysis comparing L2 RAMSESII-SNPP and -J1 profiles collocated to ERA5 and IGRA was 
performed but results were consistent regardless of the platform. To reduce redundancy those 
results are omitted from this report. For reference, RAMSESII-SNPP Version 1 had a strong 
and  persistent  cold  bias  throughout  the  troposphere  ranging  from  -1  to  -10  Kelvin  and  a 
consistent dry bias throughout the troposphere ranging from -5 to -40%.  

3.1 L2 RAMSESII-SNPP T and H2O Profile Retrieval Yield 

6 

 
 
 
 
 Fig. 1 compares the zonal mean vertical cross-section of RAMSESII-SNPP retrieval yields 
(%)  for  QC=0  (best  retrievals),  QC=1  (good  retrievals),  QC=0  or  1  for  scientific  retrievals 
when combining the two. The percentage of QC = 2 cases (not suggested for scientific use) is 
also shown. The top row is for temperature and the bottom row is for specific humidity (H2O). 
The yield is the percentage of retrievals that are flagged by QC divided by the total observation 
count; therefore, the yields of QC=0 or 1 plus the yields of QC=2 equal 100%.  

Figure 1. Zonal mean vertical cross-section of RAMSESII-SNPP retrieval yields as a percentage of a 
number of retrievals with quality flag QC=0 (best), QC=1 (good), QC=2 (not for scientific use), and QC=0 or 
1 (good for scientific use) with respect to a total number of retrievals. The data depicts one day (Jan. 1, 2015) 
of global zonal mean results. The dashed lines are the local lapse-rate tropopause following the WMO 
definition. 

RAMSESII-SNPP retained about 20% of retrievals with the highest quality control (QC 
=0). Less than 30% of all retrievals were flagged as poor (QC = 2) between 60oN and 60oS. 
More  retrievals  were  flagged  as  poor  at  the  poles,  particularly  in  the  southern  hemisphere 
where  upwards  of  60%  of  all  retrievals  were  rejected.  Yields  were  relatively  constant  as  a 
function of altitude.   

3.2 Evaluation of L2 T and H2O Profiles and TCWV Using Collocated In-situ Radiosonde 

Observations 

Two sets of independent in-situ field campaign datasets, IGRA (predominantly over land) 
and  AWI  (over  the  ocean),  are  used  to  validate  the  T  and  H2O  profiles  as  well  as  TCWV 
retrieved by RAMSESII-SNPP using the definitions in eqns. (1-4).  

3.2.1  Comparison with IGRA Data 
Fig. 2a shows the IGRA stations (black) and the successfully collocated RAMSESII-SNPP 
profiles  (red  dots)  during  January  2015.  Europe  had  the  highest  density  of  collocations 
followed by the southwestern United States. Fig. 2b shows the histogram of the distance (black 
line) and time differences in minutes (blue  line) over Europe while  Fig. 2c shows the same 
results but over the tropics. Most matchups occurred within 5-10 km of the station and less 
than 1 hour. 

7 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 Figure 2. (a) The IGRA stations (black dots) and successfully collocated RAMSESII-SNPP profiles (red dots, 
total 15212 profiles) within 3-hour, 50-km searching criteria for January 2015; (b) histogram of the distance 
(lower x-axis, black) and  time differences in minutes (upper x-axis, blue) from the collocated records over 
Europe; (c) over the tropics (30o N–30o S). 

Fig. 3 shows the T bias (left), RMSE (2nd column), the RMSE skill score with respect to 
the first guess (right) by geographical region (rows) for RAMSESII-SNPP (black), AIRS v7 
IR  Only  (blue),  and  AIRS  v7  IR+MW  (red).  The  lighter  lines  are  the  corresponding  first 
guesses. Note that AIRS v7 retrievals were independently collocated to the IGRA sondes by 
following the QC flags and satellite sampling of AIRS products. Regardless of the geographical 
location, RAMSESII-SNPP had a warm bias in the UTLS of about 1 kelvin and a cold bias in 
the lower to mid-troposphere ranging from 1 to 2 kelvin. The biases are much improved from 
the RAMSESII v1 but are still larger than the AIRS v7. Furthermore, the RAMSESII-SNPP 
bias was always larger in magnitude than its corresponding first guess (MERRA2) suggesting 
little to no improvement from the FG. The RAMSESII-SNPP RMSE ranged from 1.5 to 3 K, 
another  significant  improvement  from  version  1.  The  RAMSESII-SNPP  RMSE  was  larger 
than the AIRS v7 RMSE, except in the lower troposphere between 60-90oN where the RMSE 
ranged from 2 to 3 K but was smaller than the AIRS v7 RMSE. Regardless of the metric, the 
RAMSESII-SNPP had no skill with respect  to the first guess of temperature suggesting the 
final retrieval increases the error overall compared to the first guess, confirming the bias and 
RMSE results.   

8 

 
 
  
 
 Figure 3. Compared to IGRA radiosondes, RAMSES (black) and AIRS v7 (blue for IR and red for IR+MW) 
temperature bias (left column) and RMSE (middle column) in Europe (top row), 60-90oN, the tropics (30o N–
S), 30-60oS, and 60-90oS (bottom row). Lighter lines are the corresponding first guesses (FG). The right 
column shows the retrieval score defined as 1 –  RMSERAMSES/RMSEFG, so negative values (shaded in gray) 
indicate RAMSESII-SNPP retrieval has larger RMSE compared to its FG, and positive values indicate better 
performances of RAMSES compared to FG. 

Fig. 4 is the same as Fig. 3 but for H2O profiles in units of % and Fig. 5 shows these results 
in units of g/kg. RAMSESII-SNPP had a slight dry bias between the surface and 900 hPa in 
Europe. Regardless of the geographical location, RAMSESII-SNPP had a wet bias between 
900 hPa and the tropopause that was typically less than 20%, except in the Tropics where it 

9 

 
 
 
 exceeded  40%.  Similar  to  the  temperature  results,  the  specific  humidity  bias  was  generally 
larger than AIRS v7. RAMSESII-SNPP H2O RMSE ranged from 20-60% and was generally 
comparable to AIRS v7 H2O RMSE in the mid-troposphere. The RMSE was the largest in the 
tropical upper troposphere, exceeding 100%. Generally, RAMSESII-SNPP had a skill score of 
0  suggesting  the  final  retrieval  did  not  deviate  from  the  first  guess,  except  in  the  lower 
troposphere where the skill was negative suggesting an increase in the error with respect to the 
first guess (MERRA2).  

Figure 4. Compared to IGRA radiosondes, RAMSES (black) and AIRS v7 (blue for IR and red for IR+MW) 
H2O bias (left column) and RMSE (middle column) in Europe (top row), 60-90oN, the tropics (30o N–S), 30-

10 

 
 
 
 60oS,  and  60-90oS  (bottom  row).  Lighter  lines  are  the  corresponding  first  guesses  (FG).  The  right  column 
shows  the  retrieval  score  defined  as  1  –    RMSERAMSES/RMSEFG,  so  that  negative  values  (shaded  in  gray) 
indicate RAMSESII-SNPP retrieval has  a  larger RMSE compared to  its  FG, and a positive  value indicates 
better performances of RAMSES compared to FG. 

Figure 5. Same as Fig. 4, but with bias and RMSE reported in g/kg compared to IGRA. 

11 

 
 
 
 3.2.2  Comparison with AWI Data 
In  this  section,  RAMSESII-SNPP  is  compared  to  radiosondes  from  the  AWI  Polarstern 
ship. Fig. 6a shows all collocated RAMSESII-SNPP profiles (color-coded by surface types, 
total 76) compared to all radiosondes from AWI Polarstern 2013-2019 (black circles), with a 
temporal tolerance of ±3 hours and a spatial tolerance of 50 km radius. Eventually, we have 38 
collocated  profiles  for  2015  and  64  collocated  profiles  for  2019,  summing  to  102  total 
collocated profiles. Fig. 6b shows the histograms of the distances (black) and time differences 
(blue) of collocated datasets. Most of the collocated profiles were a within 10 km radius of the 
station. 

Figure 4. (a) The AWI Polarstern records 2013-2019 (black dots) and successfully collocated RAMSESII-
SNPP profiles (color-coded by surface types, total 76 profiles) within 3-hour, 50-km searching criteria for 
Jan/Apr/Jul/Oct of 2013 and 2015; (b) histogram of the distance (lower x-axis, black) and time differences in 
minutes (upper x-axis, blue) from the collocated records. 

Fig. 7 shows the T (top row) and H2O (bottom row) biases (left column) and RMSE (middle 
column)  compared  to  radiosondes  from  the  AWI  Polarstern  cruises  for  RAMSESII-SNPP 
(black), AIRS v7 IR Only (blue) and AIRS v7 IR+MW (red). The lighter lines represent the 
corresponding first guess.  Similar to the results using IGRA, RAMSESII-SNPP had a warm 
bias of less than 1 K in the stratosphere and an old bias of less than 2 K in the lower to mid-
troposphere.  The  magnitude  of  the  bias  was  larger  in  the  RAMSESII-SNPP  final  retrieval 
than in its first guess and AIRS v7. RAMSESII-SNPP RMSE was less than 3  K but much 
larger than the 1-2 kelvin RMSE from AIRS v7.  

12 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Figure  5. Compared to collocated radiosondes from the AWI Polarstern cruises during  Jan/Apr/Jul/Oct of 
2013 and 2015, temperature (top row) and H2O (middle row in relative percentage and bottom row in g/kg) 
bias and RMSE for RAMSES and ARIS v7 (IR in blue and IR+MW in red). The lighter lines represent the 
corresponding first guess.  

The  RAMSESII-SNPP  H2O  bias  was  larger  than  AIRS  v7  but  remained  less  than  50%, 
except in the stratosphere where the bias exceeded 100%. The RAMSESII-SNPP RMSE was 
comparable to the first guess and slightly larger than AIRS v7.  Results were fairly similar to 
the IGRA results; however, any differences could be due to sampling differences (e.g., AWI 
sampled predominantly over the ocean compared to IGRA which is predominantly over land).  
Fig.  8  compares  the  TCWV  between  collocated  RAMSESII-SNPP  retrievals  and 
radiosondes from the AWI Polarstern cruises. Note that only limited data points over high 
latitude  oceans  were  available  after  applying  collocation,  the  results  for  which  may  not 
represent  the  total  precipitable  water  retrieval  performance  in  other  regions.    The  total 
precipitable  water  was  highly  correlated  between  RAMSESII-SNPP  and  AWI.  Generally, 
RAMSESII-SNPP underestimated the low total precipitable water values and overestimated 
the high values.  

13 

 
 
 
 
 
 Figure 6. Compared to radiosondes from the AWI Polarstern cruises, (a) the collocated RAMSES total 
precipitable water and (b) the differences of total precipitable water for RAMSES–AWI on the map. (c) the 
scatter plot of RAMSES vs. AWI total precipitable water and (d) the scatter plot of RAMSES vs. (RAMSES-
AWI) total precipitable water vapor. 

3.3 Evaluation of L2 T and H2O Profiles Using Collocated ERA5 Reanalysis 

3.3.1  Global Mean Retrieval Performance 
Results presented in this section highlight the global mean bias and RMSE for RAMSESII-
SNPP, with  respect  to  the  ERA5,  and  the  yield  by  quality  control  flag  for  temperature  and 
specific humidity profiles.  

Fig. 9 shows the global mean bias (left), RMSE (2nd column), skill score (3rd column), and 
yield (4th column) for temperature aggregated by month (rows) and QC (color) for RAMSESII-
SNPP. Here, the upper row shows results for January 2015 and the lower row shows results 
for July 2015, respectively. RAMSESII-SNPP had a warm bias in the UTLS and a cold bias in 
the lower to the mid-troposphere. The bias was generally less than 2 K and was smallest when 
QC=0, except in the UTLS during January 2015. The RMSE was generally less than 3 K and 
was  smallest  when  QC=0  and  largest  when  QC=2.  Both  the  bias  and  RMSE  suggest  vast 
improvement from the earlier version of RAMSESII. However, similar to the IGRA results, 
RAMSESII-SNPP showed no skill in the final retrieval compared to its first guess MERRA2 
(all levels with skill score <0). The majority of profiles (between 50 and 75%) had a QC of 0 
or  1  while  less  than  30%  had  a  QC  =  2;  this  is  consistent  with  Fig.  1,  and  the  results  are 
consistent across months. 

Fig. 10 is the same as Fig. 9 but for specific humidity (as a percent deviation from ERA5). 
Throughout the profile, RAMSESII-SNPP had a wet bias that was less than 10% in the lower 
to mid-troposphere, between 10-20% in the mid to upper-troposphere, and less than 10% in 

14 

 
 
 
 
 
 Figure 8: Global mean temperature bias (left), RMSE (middle), and yield (right) for RAMSESII-SNPP 
aggregated by QC (QC = 0 is green, QC = 0 or 1 is orange, and QC = 2 is red)) and month (January 2015 top and 
July 2015 bottom). 

Figure 7: Global mean specific humidity bias (left), RMSE (middle), and yield (right) for RAMSESII-SNPP 
aggregated by QC (QC = 0 is green, QC = 0 or 1 is orange, and QC = 2 is red)) and month (January 2015 top and

the UTLS. Although the magnitude of the bias was generally smaller when QC=2 the RMSE 

15 

 
 was smallest when QC=0 from the surface to upper air at 500 hPa. Unlike the skill score for 
temperature, the skill score for specific humidity was predominantly 0 between 800 and 300 
hPa, which suggests that the final retrieval relaxes to its first guess in this altitude range. This 
is comparable to the results from IGRA (Fig. 4 right column). RAMSESII-SNPP redoes have 
some skill between 300 hPa and 200 hPa and no skill near the surface, where the final retrieval 
increased the error compared to the first guess. Again, the majority of the profiles had a QC = 
0 or 1 while less than 30% had a QC = 2. Results were comparable across months.   

Although not shown, the same analysis was applied to RAMSESII-J1. The bias and RMSE 

were comparable for RAMSESII-J1 and RAMSESII-SNPP.  

3.3.2  Zonal Mean Retrieval Performance 
Results presented here will be aggregated by 5o latitude bins (x-axis). Fig. 11 shows the 
zonal mean temperature bias (top left), RMSE (top right), yield (bottom left), and skill score 
(bottom right) for RAMSESII-SNPP aggregated by QC (columns), and month (rows).  Similar 
to  the  global  mean  bias,  RAMSESII-SNPP  had  a  cold  bias  from  surface  to  600  hPa,  and  a 
warm bias from 400 to 100 hPa, regardless of the latitude zone. However, the magnitude of 
the bias was larger in the tropics for QC = 0 or 1. In addition, in July 2015 the RAMSESII-
SNPP magnitude of bias was the largest in the Antarctic (70–90oS), however, the sampling is 
poor (Fig. 1) and so the measure of the mean bias may be skewed. Similar to the global mean 
results, the RMSE was largest when QC=2 suggesting the quality control flags are properly 
throwing out bad retrievals. When QC=0, the RMSE was the smallest in the tropics between 
600 and 400 hPa and the largest between 200 and 100 hPa and near the surface. The magnitude 
of the RMSE was slightly larger for QC=0 or 1. Similar to the bias results, July 2015 had a 
large RMSE in the Antarctic 70-90oN, too, due to great challenges in retrievals when there is 
less thermal contrast. Otherwise, results were  consistent between months.  When QC=0, the 
RAMSESII-SNPP yield was 50-60% during January around 60-70oS while the yield was about 
10-20% around 60-70oN. In July the yield increased to 60-70% in the north. RAMSESII-SNPP 
flagged  the  majority  of  profiles  in  the  poles  as  bad  (QC  =2)  during  January  while  more 
retrievals  pass  the  quality  control  in  July  in  these  regions.  A  negative  score  (blue)  means 
RAMSESII-SNPP increased the MSE relative to the first guess (MERRA2) assuming ERA5 
is the true reference. Similar to the global mean results, the skill score was generally negative 
for RAMSESII-SNPP suggesting that the final retrieval increases the MSE overall compared 
to  the  first  guess  (MERRA2).  There  was  a  small  region  near  the  tropical  tropopause  that 
suggests some skill in the final retrieval. These results are consistent with the results compared 
to IGRA and AWI despite the much fewer samples used.  

Fig.  12  is  the  same  as  Fig  11  but  for  specific  humidity.  RAMSESII-SNPP  was 
predominantly wet compared to ERA5 throughout the profile. There were regions of strong 
dry biases, particularly in the tropical stratosphere and near the surface at the poles. The wet 
biases were largest in UTLS of the mid-latitude to high-latitude regions and between 400 and 
200 hPa in the tropics. The biases were particularly small (less than 10%) in the mid- to lower- 

16 

 
 
 troposphere in the  tropics.  The bias when QC=2  was predominantly dry and  the  magnitude 
was smaller, similar to the global mean results. RAMSESII-SNPP RMSE was the largest in 
the tropical stratosphere and the lowest near the surface in the tropics. Generally, the RMSE 
was less than 50% when QC=0 or 1. The yield was low in the southern hemisphere where the 
majority of the profiles were flagged as QC = 2, particularly during January 2015. QC = 0 or 
1 had the largest yield and was consistent throughout the profile. The skill score was close to 
0 for most latitude zones between 800 and 300 hPa, suggesting the final retrieval sticks close 
to the first guess – just as shown from the analysis on IGRA and AWI sonde observations. The 
skill was highest when QC = 0. Indeed, there were positive skill scores between 400 and 200 
hPa  in  the  tropics  suggesting  an  overall  reduction  in  the  error  compared  to  the  first  guess 
(MERRA2). Strong negative skill scores occurred near the surface for all latitude zones and in 
the tropical stratosphere.  

Figure 9: Zonal mean temperature bias (top left), RMSE (top right), yield (bottom left), and skill score (bottom 
right)  for RAMSESII-SNPP aggregated by QC (columns) and month (rows).

 Figure 10: Same as Fig. 11 but for Specific Humidity. 

3.3.3  Global Maps of T and H2O 
Fig.  13  shows  the  global  map  of  the  5-day  mean  bias  (left)  and  RMSE  (right)  for 
Temperature for QC = 0 or 1 by month (column) and pressure (300, 500, 700, and 850 hPa). 
Similar to the global mean results, the bias moved from warm in the upper atmosphere to cold 
in  the  lower  part  of  the  atmosphere.  At  300  hPa  the  RAMSESII-SNPP  bias  was  warm  and 
large, generally greater than 3 K across all locations. At 500 hPa, the bias was predominantly 
warm but typically less than 2 K between 60oS and 60oN. At the high latitudes, the bias was 
generally  cold.  At  700  hPa  the  bias  shifted  to  be  predominantly  cold,  however,  the  poles 
generally  had  a  warm  bias.  At  850  hPa,  the  bias  was  typically  around  -2  kelvin  although 
pockets of warm biases in the Southern Ocean were observed. The RMSE was largest at 300 
hPa,  particularly  over  Antarctica  during  July,  while  the  RMSE  was  smallest  at  500  hPa, 
typically less than 1 kelvin. 

Fig.  14  is  the  same  as  Fig.  13  but  for  specific  humidity.  RAMSESII-SNPP  had  a 
predominant wet bias for all pressure levels. At 300 hPa, there were some dry biases in high 
elevations,  like  the  Himalayas.  At  700  hPa,  there  were  dry  regions,  predominantly  in  the 

18 

 
 
 tropics, between 30pN and 30oS over both land and ocean. At 850 hPa, the bias was dry in the 
northern  high  latitudes  during  January  and  in  the  southern  high  latitudes  during  July. 
Furthermore, the dry regions tended to be overland. The RMSE was the smallest closer to the 
surface and highest near the top of the atmosphere. At 300 hPa in July, there were regions of 
high RMSE over the Himalayas that corresponded to regions of high biases. At 700 hPa, there 
were  high  biases  in  the  tropics  as  well  as  pockets  of  high  biases  over  the  Southern  Ocean, 
particularly in July. At 850 hPa, the smallest RMSEs occurred in the tropics, between 30oN 
and 30oS, and were observed over both land and ocean.   

Figure 13: Global maps of bias (left) and RMSE (right) for temperature with QC = 0 or 1 aggregated by pressure level (rows) and month 
(columns). 

Figure 14: Same as Fig. 13 but for Specific Humidity. 

3.3.4  T and H2O Retrieval Performance Dependence on Climate Regimes 

19 

 
 
 Results presented here illustrate the dependence of RAMSESII-SNPP T and H2O retrieval 
performance on climate regimes using ERA5 as truth. Specifically, the instantaneous retrievals 
are aggregated by scene type (land versus ocean), surface temperature, and total column water 
vapor concentrations.  

Fig. 15 shows the temperature bias, RMSE, skill score, and yield for RAMSESII-SNPP 
aggregated by land and ocean (color), QC (column), and month (row). ‘Land’ was defined as 
mw_land_frac=1 and ‘Ocean’ was defined as mw_sea_frac=1. When QC=0, the magnitude of 
the bias was smallest for ocean scenes, generally  less than half a kelvin.  When QC=0 or 1, 
there was a cold bias between the surface and 600 hPa and a warm bias above for both ocean 
and land scenes. The warm bias above 600 hPa, however, was smallest over land. When QC = 
0 or 1 the RMSE was larger for ocean scenes, especially above 600 hPa. Indeed, the RMSE 
was  generally  never  less  than  2  for  ocean  scenes,  regardless  of  the  QC  flag.  Similar  to  the 
global mean results, the final retrieval had no skill compared to the first guess. More retrievals 

Figure 15: Global mean temperature bias (a), RMSE (b), and yield (c) aggregated 
by land versus ocean.  Rows represent months and columns represent the quality 
control flag. 

20 

 
 were flagged with QC=0 over land than ocean. Indeed, almost 70% of all retrievals over land 
had a QC flag of 0.   

Fig.  16  is  the  same  as  Fig.  15  but  aggregated  by  surface  skin  temperature  (color).  The 
purple  color  represents  cooler  scenes  while  the  red  color  represents  warmer  scenes  and  the 
orange  represents  scenes  in  between  (moderate).  The  magnitude  of  the  bias  was  generally 
smaller for retrievals over cooler scenes than warmer scenes. Indeed, the magnitude of the bias 
grew with increasing surface skin temperatures. The pattern for RMSE was less clear. There 
was some skill for retrievals over warm scenes near the top of the atmosphere but generally, 
the retrieval had little to no skill over the first guess. Although cooler scenes tended to have 
high yields of QC = 0 they also threw out more retrievals than scenes over warm temperatures.   

Figure 16: Global mean temperature bias (a), RMSE (b), and yield (c) aggregated 
by surface skin temperature.  Rows represent months and columns represent the 
quality control flag. 

Fig.  17  is  the  same  as  Fig  15.  but  aggregated  by  TCWV  (color).  Generally,  drier 
environments had a smaller bias compared to those that were wet (red). This was particularly 

21 

 
 
 true above 600 hPa. In the UTLS, the temperature RMSE grew as the TCWV grew. There was, 
however, some skill in the final retrieval in the stratosphere when retrieving in extremely wet 
environments  (when  the  TCWV  was  greater  than  28  mm).  Drier  environments  had  higher 
yields for QC=0.  

Figure 17: Global mean temperature bias (a), RMSE (b), and yield (c) aggregated 
by total column water vapor.  Rows represent months and columns represent the 
quality control flag. 

Fig. 18 shows the specific humidity bias, RMSE,  skill score, and yield for RAMSESII-
SNPP aggregated by land versus ocean (color), QC (column), and month (row). The pattern 
was quite strong. Both the bias and RMSE were smaller when retrieving over land than over 
the ocean. The skill, however, was approximately the same regardless of the scene type. The 
majority of retrievals were flagged with high quality over land while the majority of retrievals 
were flagged with QC=0 or 1 over the ocean. About 25% of the retrievals over the ocean were 
flagged as bad (QC=2) while less than 20% over land were flagged as bad.  

Fig. 19 is the same as Fig. 18 but aggregated by surface skin temperature. The pattern was 
less clear. Colder  environments, however, had smaller biases  and RMSEs between 400  and 
200 hPa while warm environments did better near the surface. Regardless of the surface skin

aggregated by land and ocean.  Rows represent months and columns represent the

temperature,  the  skill  score  was  still  close  to  0,  except  for  the  colder  environments.  In  the 
colder environments, the skill score deviated from the 0-line suggesting a change from the first 
guess. The deviation, however, was negative suggesting the final retrieval was worse than the 
first guess. About 30% of the retrievals over cold environments passed with the high-quality 
control flag (QC=0).   

Fig. 20 is the same as Fig. 18 but aggregated by total column water vapor. Between 600 
and 200 hPa, the magnitude of the bias grew with increasing TCWV. The RMSE, however, 
exhibited a different pattern and was smallest when retrieving in high TCWV environments 
and largest when retrieving in low TCWV environments. The skill was close to 0 except for 
near the surface and when retrieving  in drier environments. Similar to the cold surface skin 
temperature, the skill score suggests the final retrieval deviates from the first guess, particularly 
in cold and dry environments, and that deviation results in an increase in the error. About 30-

 40% of the retrievals in dry environments had a QC=0 flag, while only 25% had a QC=0 flag 
in wet environments.

4.  Summary and Conclusions 

The  goal  of  this  report  is  to  provide  an  initial  testing  analysis  aimed  at  evaluating  the 
general  quality  of  the  retrieval  including  the  retrieval  yields,  the  bias,  and  RMSE  of  key 
parameters  such  as  temperature  (T)  and  water  vapor/specific  humidity  (H2O),  and  initial 
assessments of single level and total column variables. The possible connections of T/ H2O 
bias  and  RMSE  to  other  parameters  such  as  land  fraction,  surface  temperature,  and  total 
column water vapor were explored. IGRA/AWI radiosondes and ERA5 provided truth data for 
the comparison of retrievals from RAMSESII-SNPP and -J1. The previous version (v1) had a 
persistent cold bias throughout the troposphere ranging from -1 to -7 kelvin and a consistent 

24 

 
 
 dry bias throughout the troposphere ranging from -5 to -40%. Below is a brief summary of the 
key results from version 3. 

Temperature  results  were  similar  regardless  of  using  IGRA/AWI  or  ERA5.  The  results 
discussed here are generalized across all truth data sets. RAMSESII v3 has greatly improved 
from version 1. In v3, RAMSESII-SNPP had a cold bias between the surface and 600 hPa that 
was less than 1 kelvin for QC<2 and a warm bias less than 1 K in the UTLS region. The largest 
biases occurred in the Tropical upper troposphere. The RMSE was less than 3 K for QC<2 and 
was  highest  in  the  Tropics  between  300  and  100  hPa.  Although  a  vast  improvement  was 
observed, AIRS v7 still had smaller errors compared to RAMSESII. RAMSESII-SNPP skill 
remained low suggesting little improvement of the first guess (MERRA2). Retrievals over land 
had smaller RMSEs than those over the ocean while dry and cold environments tended to have 
smaller errors. Less than 20% of all retrievals had a QC=0 while around 60-75% had a QC=0 
or 1. Bad retrievals were flagged roughly 25% of the time and were particularly common at 
the poles. Results comparing RAMSESII-SNPP and -J1 were remarkably similar (not shown).  
Similar to the temperature results, specific humidity results were similar regardless of using 
IGRA/AWI  or  ERA5.  The  results  discussed  here  are  generalized  across  all  truth  data  sets. 
RAMSESII version 3 specific humidity has improved from version 1. Generally, a wet bias 
was observed throughout  the profile ranging from 5-10% in the lower-mid troposphere and 
increasing to 25% around 400-200 hPa. The largest wet biases occurred in the tropics between 
400 and 200 hPa and in the stratospheric mid-latitude regions. RAMSESII-SNPP RMSE was 
generally less than 60% and was largest in the tropics between 600 and 100 hPa and smallest 
near the surface in the tropics. Furthermore, the RMSE was smallest over land than in the ocean 
and when retrieving over warm and wet environments.  RAMSESII-SNPP stuck close to the 
first guess resulting in 0 skill for most of the profile. There was, however, skill in the tropics 
between 400-200 hPa, a region where the first guess had extremely large errors. AIRS v7 had 
comparable errors in the lower to mid-troposphere over the tropics and southern mid-latitudes. 
RAMSESII-SNPP, however, had larger errors compared to AIRS v7 between 400 and 200 hPa 
regardless of the region. This corresponds to the large errors observed in the RAMSESII-SNPP 
first guess suggesting that even though there was an improvement from the first guess, it was 
not enough to  counter  the poor starting point of MERRA2. Results comparing RAMSESII-
SNPP and -J1 (not shown) were comparable with ignorable differences in yield. In particular, 
J1 had slightly larger yields for QC = 0 than SNPP.
